ugf_response: First we see that the table shows two possible ways to ask for money, high and low, and two possible times when many want the thing, good time and bad time, and the numbers that tell us the number for each pair. In a good time the high how much of money asked gives the biggest number bigger than the middle number and the small number. In a bad time the low how much of money asked gives the biggest number bigger than the other two. Because the best how much of money asked changes with the time when many want the thing no how much of money asked is always the best. So we can not pick a how much of money asked just by saying it is always better we need another way to choose that looks at the time when many want the thing. No how much of money asked is always the best so we need a way that looks at the good time or the bad time to pick the right how much of money asked.

english_query: If she takes the medicine, then her headache goes away. She takes the medicine. Therefore, her headache goes away. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.
ugf_response: First, we look at the first idea. It says if she takes the sick fix, then her head pain goes away. This sets up a clear path between two things. The first thing is taking the sick fix. The second thing is the head pain going away. The idea tells us that the first thing leads to the second thing. Next, we look at the second idea. It says that she takes the sick fix. This tells us that the first part of the path has happened. Since the first part happened, and the first idea says the second part must happen when the first part happens, we can see that the second part must also happen. We do not need to guess. The path is clear. If the first thing happens, the second thing happens. The first thing did happen. So the second thing must happen. So, it makes sense to say her head pain goes away. We can imagine a small story to try this out. If I say if it rains, the grass gets wet, and then I say it is raining, you would say the grass is wet. It is the same kind of thinking here. Nothing stops the end part from happening. So the last idea follows for sure from the earlier ideas. follows

english_query: Either the door is open or the door is locked. The door is not open. Therefore, the door is locked. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.

Since the first idea says it must be one or the other, and we know it is not the first one, it has to be the second one. So this means the door is locked. The last idea says the door is locked. This follows for sure. follows

english_query: If the river is high, then the bank is flooded. If the bank is flooded, then the bank is flooded. Therefore, if the river is high, then the bank is flooded. Is this argument valid or invalid? Explain your reasoning.

The second idea does not add any new join, it just repeats that a bank with a lot of water stays with a lot of water. The last idea is exactly the same as the first idea. Because the first idea is given as true, the last idea must also be true. Nothing can make the first idea not true. So anyone can agree with both earlier ideas and still have to agree that if the river is high then the bank has a lot of water. So the last idea follows for sure. follows
